up.method: calculated: 95% of 0.05 g/100 mL (=0.5 g/L =8.325 mmol/L using MW urea 60.06 g/mol) urea hydrolyzed over 24 h incubation = 7.909 mmol/L consumed / 24 h = 0.3295 mmol/L/h

growth_records.cite: Liu et al. Functional gene-guided enrichment plus in situ microsphere cultivation enables isolation of new crucial ureolytic bacteria from the rumen of cattle. Microbiome (2023). DOI:10.1186/s40168-023-01510-4. [PMC10105427]
growth_records.snippet: Hydrolysis of the urea added to the culture medium (0.05 g/100 ml) by the isolates ranged from 2 to 95%, with strain S99 (P. penneri) having the greatest...urea hydrolysis. All isolates with urease genes were cultured an
growth_records.notes: Strain S99 had the single highest urea hydrolysis fraction reported in text (95% of added urea). Rate calculated from: urea initial conc. 0.05 g/100mL = 0.5 g/L; MW urea=60.06 g/mol -> 8.325 mmol/L; 95% hydrolyzed over the stated 24 h batch incubation (39C, anaerobic, Methods section 'Ureolytic activity...') = 7.909 mmol/L / 24h = 0.3295 mmol/L/h. This is a volumetric batch consumption rate over the whole 10 mL culture, NOT normalized to biomass/gDW -- the paper only reports urea hydrolysis and ammonia-N production normalized per culture OD600 (arbitrary units, Supplementary Fig. 6), not per gDW, so a true specific qS could not be computed from the numbers given in text. No growth rate (mu) is reported or calculable since only an endpoint OD600 (after 24 h) was measured, not a growth time-course.

phenotypes.base_medium: Sole-substrate growth / assimilation on a defined minimal medium (substrate as C/N/energy source) — positive = the organism grows on it
